title: Product BOM Decomposition: GfS Türwächter IoT

sections.title: Market Context: Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings (NAICS 531120)
sections.content:
*Component: Product BOM Decomposition (Steps B.1–B.4). Follows the methodology in Clayton/Docs/components/product-bom-decomposition.html. Market context from 05_market_discovery.md (Market 04). Input constraints from 03_constraints.md.*

---

sections.title: NAICS Routing Check
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Product NAICS (manufacturing)** | 332321 — Metal Window and Door Manufacturing (Assa Abloy / GfS as manufacturer) |
| **Market NAICS (deployment)** | 531120 — Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings |
| **BOM applies?** | YES — the Türwächter is a physical manufactured product with full compositional structure (L4→L0). The market is a service sector, but the BOM describes the product itself, not the market's output. |
| **Output type** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring |

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
Four subsystems apply. No control subsystem is fused into the IoT module; it is listed separately because it is the primary basis for competitive differentiation in the NAICS 531120 context.

```
Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT (L5 — output type)
│
├── L4  Mechanical Guard Subsystem
│       Primary functional core. Physically blocks/releases the door handle.
│       The safety-critical subsystem — must operate at 0 V per EltVTR / EN 179 / EN 1125.
│
├── L4  Alarm / Alert Subsystem
│       Audible alarm triggered by guard motion (mechanical linkage, no power required).
│       Pre-alarm (touch warning) is an optional module within this subsystem.
│
├── L4  IoT / Wireless Monitoring Subsystem
│       Control and reporting layer. Detects guard state, alarm, and battery events.
│       Broadcasts via 868 MHz EasyWave. Battery-powered. Purely supervisory.
│
└── L4  Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem
        Housing, weather protection, and door-handle attachment hardware.
        Retrofit-only surface mount; no flush-frame integration available.
```

---

sections.title: B.2 — Full Decomposition (L4 → L0)
sections.content:
### L4: Mechanical Guard Subsystem

| Level | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| L4 | Mechanical Guard Subsystem | Blocks handle depression; releases on 45° swing |
| L3 | Handle locking mechanism | Spring-loaded guard body over door handle |
| L2 | Guard housing assembly | Structural shell + pivot integration |
| L1 | Guard body (housing shell) | Single-piece injection-molded or die-cast enclosure |
| **L0** | **Housing material** | **Design choice → alternatives exist (see B.3)** |
| L2 | Spring return assembly | Returns guard to locked position after release |
| L1 | Return spring (coil) | Maintains guard in locked position |
| **L0** | **Spring steel alloy** | No meaningful alternatives at this level — commodity |
| L1 | Spring guide pin | Stainless steel — commodity, no alternatives |
| L1 | Pivot axle | Rotational axis for 45° guard swing |
| **L0** | **Pivot material** | Stainless steel or zinc alloy — minor design choice |
| L3 | Key override module | Authorized bypass without alarm trigger |
| L2 | Lock cylinder assembly | Half-cylinder key lock |
| **L1** | **Profile half-cylinder** | **Design choice → alternatives exist (see B.3)** |
| L0 | Cylinder material | Brass or zinc alloy — cost/durability trade-off |
| L1 | Lock cam / actuator | Mechanically decouples alarm trigger on key turn |
| L1 | Cylinder retaining clip | Stainless steel — commodity |

### L4: Alarm / Alert Subsystem

| Level | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| L4 | Alarm / Alert Subsystem | Audible 95 dB alert; mechanical trigger, no power required |
| L3 | Acoustic alarm module | Generates 95 dB tone on guard activation |
| L2 | Alarm horn assembly | Transducer + resonance chamber |
| **L1** | **Acoustic transducer element** | **Design choice → alternatives exist (see B.3)** |
| **L0** | **Transducer material** | PZT ceramic (lead zirconate titanate) — for piezo variants |
| L1 | Resonance / amplification chamber | Polymer housing tuned to target frequency |
| L0 | Chamber material | ABS or polypropylene — commodity |
| L2 | Mechanical trigger linkage | Connects guard swing to alarm activation |
| L1 | Actuator arm | Transmits 45° rotation to alarm trigger |
| L1 | Trigger contact point | Spring-loaded contact; closes alarm circuit on activation |
| L0 | Contact material | Phosphor bronze — standard, no alternatives |
| L3 | Pre-alarm module (optional variant) | Warning tone on slight handle touch; ceases on release |
| L2 | Touch detection assembly | Detects incipient handle pressure |
| **L1** | **Trigger sensor** | **Design choice → alternatives exist (see B.3)** |

### L4: IoT / Wireless Monitoring Subsystem

| Level | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| L4 | IoT / Wireless Monitoring Subsystem | State reporting; no safety-critical function |
| L3 | Radio transmission module | 868.3 MHz EasyWave broadcast of guard/alarm/battery events |
| L2 | RF transmitter PCB assembly | RF transceiver + MCU on single board |
| **L1** | **RF transceiver IC** | **Design choice (frequency + protocol) → see B.3** |
| L0 | Semiconductor substrate | Silicon — commodity |
| L1 | PCB substrate | Standard FR4 — commodity |
| L0 | PCB material | Glass-fiber epoxy (FR4) — commodity |
| **L1** | **Antenna element** | **Design choice → see B.3** |
| L0 | Antenna material | Copper trace or silver-ink on substrate — commodity |
| L1 | Microcontroller (MCU) | State machine; event detection and TX scheduling |
| L0 | MCU material | Silicon — commodity |
| L1 | Passive components | Capacitors, resistors, inductors |
| L0 | Passive materials | Ceramic (MLCC caps), metal film (resistors) — commodity |
| L3 | Battery / power module | Power supply for IoT layer only |
| L2 | Battery holder assembly | Retains and connects replaceable cell |
| L1 | Battery contact clips | Nickel-plated steel spring contacts — commodity |
| L1 | Battery compartment housing | Polypropylene — commodity |
| **L1** | **Primary battery cell (9V block)** | **Design choice (chemistry) → see B.3** |
| **L0** | **Battery chemistry** | **Design choice → alternatives exist (see B.3)** |

### L4: Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem

| Level | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| L4 | Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem | Device housing + door-handle attachment |
| L3 | Outer enclosure module | Environmental protection; IP class; colour (RAL 6029) |
| L2 | Device housing assembly | Front cover + back plate |
| **L1** | **Front cover** | **Design choice (material) → see B.3** |
| **L0** | **Cover material** | **Design choice → alternatives exist (see B.3)** |
| L1 | Back plate | Structural rear panel |
| L1 | LED indicator window | Status LED diffuser |
| L0 | Lens material | Acrylic or polycarbonate — minor choice |
| L3 | Mounting interface module | Attaches device to door handle or panic bar |
| L2 | Base mounting bracket | Clamp or screw-fix to door handle |
| **L1** | **Mounting bracket body** | **Design choice (material + method) → see B.3** |
| L0 | Bracket material | Zinc die-cast or steel sheet — design choice |
| L1 | Anti-tamper fasteners | Stainless steel — commodity |
| L2 | Glass-frame adapter plate (Art. 991170) | Optional; for glass-framed doors |
| L1 | Adapter plate body | Aluminum or zinc alloy |
| L1 | Tamper-evident label / seal | Polyester film — commodity |

---

sections.title: B.3 — Alternatives at Every Level
sections.content:
### Housing Material (L0, under L4 Mechanical Guard Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS thermoplastic | ~40% | Stable | MEDIUM | Digikey enclosure materials guide; industry teardown analysis |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | ~35% | Stable | MEDIUM | FOACHI Hardware materials comparison 2024; GfS uses zinc alloy |
| Polycarbonate | ~15% | Growing | MEDIUM | Polycase enclosure material guide (PC preferred for outdoor/IP-rated variants) |
| Aluminum alloy | ~10% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |

**Market relevance (NAICS 531120):** Zinc die-cast provides the premium aesthetic expected in commercial office lobbies and common areas. ABS is standard for back-of-house and service corridor exits.

---

### Key Override Cylinder Type (L1, under L4 Mechanical Guard Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Profile half-cylinder (European DIN standard) | ~60% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in EU commercial door hardware; GfS uses this standard |
| Oval cylinder | ~20% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; common in legacy EU buildings |
| No key override (alarm-only) | ~15% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate; lower-security / low-maintenance applications |
| Digital / electronic key (NFC/Bluetooth access) | ~5% | Growing | LOW | Emerging; not available on Türwächter today |

**Market relevance (NAICS 531120):** Multi-tenant office buildings require key override — building manager must bypass alarms during move-in/move-out operations. Profile half-cylinder is standard; keyway must match building's master key system.

---

### Acoustic Transducer Type (L1, under L4 Alarm Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric (PZT) transducer | ~70% | Stable | HIGH | Straits Research Buzzer Market 2024: piezo segment dominates; DataIntelo Alarm Buzzer Market confirms piezo leadership |
| Electromagnetic (moving-iron) horn | ~20% | Declining | HIGH | Legacy technology; lower power efficiency; still present in older alarm products |
| MEMS audio transducer | ~5% | Growing | LOW | Commercially available but not yet mainstream in safety alarms |
| Mechanical spring striker (bell) | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Legacy only; no meaningful commercial new deployment |

---

### Pre-Alarm Trigger Sensor (L1, under L4 Alarm Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical contact pressure switch | ~55% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; GfS pre-alarm uses mechanical contact |
| Capacitive proximity sensor | ~35% | Growing | LOW | Increasingly used in modern alarm guards for lower false-alarm rates |
| Optical (IR) proximity | ~10% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; niche application |

---

### Wireless Protocol / Frequency (L3 radio module, under L4 IoT Subsystem)

This is the single most commercially significant BOM-level choice for NAICS 531120 deployments due to Constraint C6 (EasyWave Protocol Lock-In).

| Alternative | Share (alarm/security devices) | BAS integration | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 868 MHz sub-GHz (EasyWave, Z-Wave EU, EnOcean) | ~35% | Via proprietary gateway only | Stable | MEDIUM | EMQ IoT protocols 2024; Z-Wave EU uses 868.4 MHz band |
| BLE 5.x (Bluetooth Low Energy) | ~25% | Via BLE gateway / Matter bridge | Growing | MEDIUM | IoT protocol market: BLE accounts for 4.8B IoT endpoints globally (2024) |
| Zigbee 3.0 (2.4 GHz) | ~20% | Native in many BAS; 65% of connected lighting (Zigbee market 2024) | Growing | HIGH | 360 Research Reports Zigbee Market 2024; 2.6B devices deployed by end-2024 |
| 433 MHz ISM | ~10% | None | Declining | LOW | Legacy alarm/sensor use; phasing out in EU |
| LoRaWAN | ~5% | Via LoRaWAN NS / BMS adapter | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; growing in large campus / industrial contexts |
| Wi-Fi (802.11) | ~5% | Native IP; highest integration ease | Stable | LOW | High power draw; unsuitable for battery-only alarm devices |

**Market relevance (NAICS 531120):** European commercial office buildings use KNX (>70% market share in European BAS, Straits Research 2024) or BACnet. Neither protocol is natively supported by EasyWave. Building operators running KNX/BACnet require a GfS proprietary wireless BUS gateway (C6 constraint). Zigbee and BLE are increasingly common in new office fit-outs and would eliminate this gateway dependency — but are not available on the Türwächter today.

---

### Battery Chemistry (L0, under L4 IoT Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share (primary batteries) | IoT alarm suitability | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alkaline (zinc-manganese dioxide, 9V block) | ~55% | Good for low-activity doors; performance degrades below 0°C | Stable | HIGH | Mordor Intelligence Primary Battery Market: alkaline 63.5% market share 2025 |
| Lithium primary (LiMnO2 / LiFeS2, 9V equivalent) | ~30% | 3–5× longer life vs alkaline; stable performance at low temperature | Growing | HIGH | Fire alarm systems: 57% of commercial fire alarms (North America) use high-capacity lithium (GlobalGrowthInsights 2024) |
| Lithium rechargeable (LiPo / NiMH, with charging circuit) | ~10% | Requires charging infrastructure; unsuitable for retrofit-only surface mount | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate |
| Mains power (eliminates battery) | ~5% | Eliminates battery maintenance; requires wiring at door point (violates retrofit premise) | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |

**Market relevance (NAICS 531120):** Multi-building portfolio managers (commercial landlords) have 50–200+ monitored exit doors. Lithium batteries provide ~3–5× longer service life, dramatically reducing maintenance visits. For office buildings where some exits (stairwells, service corridors) are rarely opened, lithium is the preferred L0 choice to avoid premature battery failure.

---

### Outer Cover Material (L0, under L4 Enclosure Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share | IP suitability | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS thermoplastic | ~40% | Indoor only (no published IP rating) | Stable | MEDIUM | Digikey enclosure guide: ABS favored for indoor; generally no NEMA/IP rating |
| Polycarbonate | ~30% | IP65+ achievable; UV-stable | Growing | MEDIUM | Polycase guide: PC preferred for outdoor / high-impact / IP-rated applications |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | ~20% | IP45 achievable; premium finish | Stable | MEDIUM | FOACHI Hardware; GfS IP45 variant uses sealed zinc housing |
| Aluminum extrusion | ~10% | IP65+ achievable; premium; heavier | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |

---

### Mounting Bracket Attachment Method (L2, under L4 Enclosure Subsystem)

| Alternative | Share | Installation time | Retrofit? | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Screw-clamp to existing door handle (no drilling) | ~60% | <30 min | YES | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate; GfS primary installation method |
| Through-door bolt (mechanical fastening) | ~20% | 1–2 hrs | Partial (drilling required) | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |
| Adhesive bonding (structural adhesive) | ~10% | <15 min | YES | Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate; used for heritage/no-drill contexts |
| Rail/channel mounting (extruded profile) | ~10% | System-specific | NO (requires infrastructure) | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |

**Market relevance (NAICS 531120):** Commercial landlords need retrofit-capable installation to avoid disrupting tenants and to avoid requiring building permits for structural modifications. Screw-clamp is the dominant method and aligns with the Türwächter's core retrofit value proposition.

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Mapping (Selected)
sections.content:
### Category: Wireless Radio Module

| BOM Position | Subsystem | Function | Safety-critical | Key alternatives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L3 under IoT Subsystem | IoT / Wireless Monitoring | Broadcasts guard-state, alarm, and battery events to gateway | NO (supervisory only) | 868 MHz EasyWave, BLE 5.x, Zigbee 3.0, LoRaWAN |

**Insight (NAICS 531120):** The wireless radio module faces completely different competitive pressure than in NAICS 531120 vs. industrial contexts. In commercial office buildings, the protocol choice determines system integration cost: EasyWave requires a proprietary GfS gateway ($$$), while Zigbee/BLE would integrate natively into a BACnet/IP or Matter ecosystem that most Grade-A office buildings are moving toward post-2022. The current Türwächter locks tenants into a parallel radio sub-system — a meaningful barrier to portfolio-scale adoption by large commercial landlords.

### Category: Primary Battery Cell

| BOM Position | Subsystem | Function | Safety-critical | Key alternatives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 under Battery/Power Module (IoT Subsystem) | IoT / Wireless Monitoring | Power source for radio TX and MCU; mechanical alarm independent of battery | NO (IoT layer only; mechanical alarm functions at 0V) | 9V alkaline, 9V lithium primary, NiMH rechargeable |

**Insight (NAICS 531120):** For a commercial landlord managing 100 exit doors across 5 office buildings, battery replacement is the primary ongoing maintenance cost. At standard alkaline life (~12–18 months in moderate-activity office exits), 100-door portfolio requires ~70–100 battery replacements/year. Switching to lithium primary cells extends cycle to 3–5 years, reducing maintenance visits by ~70%. This is a BOM-level L0 choice (battery chemistry) with direct impact on total cost of ownership — the primary commercial argument for premium IoT variant adoption in this market.

---

sections.title: Market-Specific BOM Notes (NAICS 531120)
sections.content:
| BOM Level | Component | NAICS 531120 Implication | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| L3 Radio module | Wireless protocol | Office buildings use KNX/BACnet BAS — EasyWave requires proprietary gateway for integration | C6 Protocol Lock-In |
| L0 Battery chemistry | Alkaline vs. lithium | Lithium preferred for large portfolios; 3–5× longer life reduces maintenance cost | C4 Battery Life |
| L2 Mounting bracket | Attachment method | Screw-clamp (no drilling) essential for tenant lease continuity and avoiding structural permits | C5 Retrofit Surface Mount |
| L1 Profile half-cylinder | Key override | Must be masterkeyed to building's lock cylinder system — landlord specifies cylinder profile at procurement | None (standard practice) |
| L1 Acoustic transducer | Alarm volume | 95 dB is appropriate for office corridors; some tenants in premium Class-A buildings may require volume-adjustable variant | C7 Price premium |

---

*Sources used for factual claims:*
- [Straits Research Buzzer Market 2024](https://straitsresearch.com/report/buzzer-market) — piezo market dominance
- [DataIntelo Alarm Buzzer Market](https://dataintelo.com/report/alarm-buzzer-market) — alarm buzzer segment breakdown
- [EMQ IoT Protocols 2024](https://www.emqx.com/en/blog/iot-protocols-mqtt-coap-lwm2m) — protocol distribution
- [360 Research Reports Zigbee Market 2024](https://www.360researchreports.com/market-reports/zigbee-market-200913) — Zigbee device count and penetration
- [Mordor Intelligence Primary Battery Market](https://www.mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/global-primary-battery-market-industry) — alkaline 63.5% share
- [GlobalGrowthInsights Fire Alarm Battery Market 2024](https://www.globalgrowthinsights.com/market-reports/fire-alarm-battery-market-115577) — lithium adoption in commercial fire alarms
- [Straits Research KNX Products Market Europe](https://straitsresearch.com/report/knx-products-market/europe) — KNX >70% European commercial BAS market
- [Digikey Enclosure Materials Guide](https://www.digikey.com/en/articles/weighing-the-pros-and-cons-of-enclosure-materials) — ABS vs PC vs metal enclosure properties
- [Polycase Enclosure Material Guide](https://www.polycase.com/techtalk/materials/enclosure-material.html) — polycarbonate for IP-rated applications
- [FOACHI Hardware Materials Comparison](https://www.foachihardware.com/blogs/door-hardware-materials-comparison) — zinc alloy vs stainless steel vs aluminum in door hardware

rows.Value: 332321 — Metal Window and Door Manufacturing (Assa Abloy / GfS as manufacturer)
YES — the Türwächter is a physical manufactured product with full compositional structure (L4→L0). The market is a service sector, but the BOM describes the product itself, not the market's output.
Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

rows.Name: Mechanical Guard Subsystem
rows.Role: Blocks handle depression; releases on 45° swing

rows.Name: Handle locking mechanism
rows.Role: Spring-loaded guard body over door handle

rows.Name: Spring return assembly
rows.Role: Returns guard to locked position after release

rows.Name: **Spring steel alloy**
rows.Role: No meaningful alternatives at this level — commodity

rows.Name: **Pivot material**
rows.Role: Stainless steel or zinc alloy — minor design choice

rows.Name: Cylinder material
rows.Role: Brass or zinc alloy — cost/durability trade-off

rows.Name: Lock cam / actuator
rows.Role: Mechanically decouples alarm trigger on key turn

rows.Name: Alarm / Alert Subsystem
rows.Role: Audible 95 dB alert; mechanical trigger, no power required

rows.Name: Acoustic alarm module
rows.Role: Generates 95 dB tone on guard activation

rows.Name: **Transducer material**
rows.Role: PZT ceramic (lead zirconate titanate) — for piezo variants

rows.Name: Resonance / amplification chamber
rows.Role: Polymer housing tuned to target frequency

rows.Name: Mechanical trigger linkage
rows.Role: Connects guard swing to alarm activation

rows.Name: Trigger contact point
rows.Role: Spring-loaded contact; closes alarm circuit on activation

rows.Name: Pre-alarm module (optional variant)
rows.Role: Warning tone on slight handle touch; ceases on release

rows.Name: Radio transmission module
rows.Role: 868.3 MHz EasyWave broadcast of guard/alarm/battery events

rows.Name: RF transmitter PCB assembly
rows.Role: RF transceiver + MCU on single board

rows.Name: Antenna material
rows.Role: Copper trace or silver-ink on substrate — commodity

rows.Name: Microcontroller (MCU)
rows.Role: State machine; event detection and TX scheduling

rows.Name: Battery / power module
rows.Role: Power supply for IoT layer only

rows.Name: Mounting interface module
rows.Role: Attaches device to door handle or panic bar

rows.Name: Base mounting bracket
rows.Role: Clamp or screw-fix to door handle

rows.Name: Bracket material
rows.Role: Zinc die-cast or steel sheet — design choice

entities.name: Mechanical Guard Subsystem
entities.description: Spring-loaded guard body over door handle; physically blocks handle depression in locked position; releases via 45° swing; operates at 0V (no power required)
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Handle locking mechanism
entities.description: Spring-loaded guard body with pivot; primary function is physical handle interlock
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Guard housing assembly
entities.description: Structural shell integrating guard body, spring return, and pivot axle
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Guard body (housing shell)
entities.description: Single-piece injection-molded or die-cast enclosure; RAL 6029 safety green standard
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Housing material
entities.description: Material from which guard housing shell is formed; determines durability, IP class, and manufacturing method
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Key override module
entities.description: Profile half-cylinder lock allowing authorized bypass of alarm trigger
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Lock cylinder assembly
entities.description: Half-cylinder key lock mechanism with cam and retaining clip
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Profile half-cylinder
entities.description: European DIN-standard half-cylinder; must match building master key system on installation
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Alarm / Alert Subsystem
entities.description: Mechanical linkage triggers 95 dB audible alarm on guard activation; operates without electrical power
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Acoustic alarm module
entities.description: Alarm horn assembly including transducer, resonance chamber, and mechanical trigger linkage
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Alarm horn assembly
entities.description: Transducer element + resonance/amplification chamber
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Acoustic transducer element
entities.description: Sound-generating element; piezoelectric on current Türwächter; triggered mechanically via linkage arm
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: IoT / Wireless Monitoring Subsystem
entities.description: Supervisory layer only; broadcasts guard-state, alarm, and battery events via 868.3 MHz EasyWave; purely additive over mechanical primary
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Radio transmission module
entities.description: 868.3 MHz EasyWave RF transmitter; range ~30 m (extendable via repeaters)
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: RF transmitter PCB assembly
entities.description: RF transceiver IC + MCU + passive components on FR4 substrate
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: RF transceiver IC
entities.description: Wireless SoC implementing EasyWave protocol on 868.3 MHz band; current product locks to GfS EasyWave
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

entities.name: Battery / power module
entities.description: Power supply for IoT layer only; mechanical alarm is power-independent

entities.name: Primary battery cell (9V block, PP3)

entities.description: Electrochemical system determining energy density, temperature range, and service life
